children.name: Certhia
children.description: Slender streak-backed birds that spiral up tree trunks like animated pieces of bark, probing crevices with a fine curved bill. They always work upward — once at the top they drop to the base of the next tree and begin again. The two Swedish species are almost identical and best separated by their songs.

children.name: Certhia familiaris
children.description: A streaky, bark-coloured bird that spirals upward around tree trunks probing crevices with a fine curved bill, then drops to the base of the next tree to start again. The commoner of Sweden's two near-identical treecreepers, best identified by song.

children.name: Certhia brachydactyla
children.description: Almost indistinguishable from the Eurasian Treecreeper except by its harder, more rhythmic song and habitat preferences, reaching the northern edge of its range in southernmost Sweden. It favours broadleaved woodland and parkland.

children.name: Certhia americana
children.description: The only treecreeper of North America; spirals up tree trunks gleaning insects, then flutters to the base of the next tree, exactly like its Old World relatives.

children.name: Certhia himalayana
children.description: A treecreeper of Himalayan and Central Asian conifer forests, distinguished by faint dark barring on the tail.

children.name: Certhia nipalensis
children.description: A high-altitude Himalayan treecreeper with warm rufous flanks and a strongly marked face.

children.name: Certhia discolor
children.description: A dark, plain-rumped treecreeper of the eastern Himalayas and hills of Southeast Asia.

children.name: Certhia hodgsoni
children.description: Split from the Eurasian Treecreeper based on song and genetics; resident in the western Himalayas.

children.name: Certhia manipurensis
children.description: A treecreeper of montane forests from northeast India to northern Southeast Asia, recently elevated to full species.

children.name: Salpornis
children.description: The spotted creepers, boldly spotted bark-foragers of Africa and India.

children.name: Salpornis spilonotus
children.description: A heavily black-and-white spotted bark-creeper of dry open woodland in India; unlike true treecreepers it does not use its tail as a prop when climbing.
